steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "In a 2023 article, ______ asserts that…" The blank is the subject of the verb "asserts." The subject is "physicist Mishkat Bhattacharya" — a title directly attached to a name, forming a single noun phrase. No comma should separate this subject from its verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — physicist Mishkat Bhattacharya,: the trailing comma falls between the subject and the verb "asserts," which is never correct. ✗
  • Choice B — physicist, Mishkat Bhattacharya: the comma after "physicist" splits the title from the name as if the title were a separate appositive, which it isn't — "physicist" here is a descriptor, not a standalone noun. ✗
  • Choice C — physicist Mishkat Bhattacharya: no commas. The title and name flow directly into the verb "asserts" without interruption, matching standard English punctuation for this construction. ✓
  • Choice D — physicist, Mishkat Bhattacharya,: both errors at once — a comma splitting title from name and a comma between the subject and verb. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
